planting.note: Plant dry tubers in late summer to early fall with the flat, rough side up about 1 inch below the soil surface in sharply drained soil; autumn cyclamen goes completely dormant in summer — plant where summer moisture will be naturally absent or where they will be sheltered from summer rain by tree canopy.

directions: Plant tubers in late summer in sharply drained, gritty, humus-rich soil under deciduous trees in shade to part shade; autumn cyclamen is a Mediterranean woodland bulb that produces charming swept-back flowers in pink, rose, or white at the most unexpected moment — emerging directly from bare earth in September and October before any leaves appear, carpeting dry shade beneath oaks and conifers with cheerful color just as the garden winds down. After flowering, the strikingly attractive silver-patterned, ivy-shaped leaves emerge and persist through winter and spring, creating a beautiful ground-level tapestry. The plant disappears completely in summer when it requires dry dormancy. It naturalizes freely from self-sown seed, expanding gradually into carpets under trees where little else will grow. Tubers slowly enlarge to saucer-size over decades and become long-lived garden heirlooms.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 4–6 inches deep in sharply drained soil in late summer; sternbergia flowers best in dry, baked conditions after a warm, dry summer — plant on slopes, raised beds, or at the base of south-facing walls where summer drainage is excellent. Bulbs resent being disturbed once established and should be left undivided for years.

directions: Plant bulbs in sharply drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; autumn daffodil (Sternbergia lutea) produces bright, clear-yellow crocus-like flowers in September and October from bare soil before the narrow, glossy, dark-green leaves emerge — making it look remarkably like a yellow crocus but actually a member of the amaryllis family. Native to the Mediterranean and Middle East, where it grows in rocky limestone soils and olive groves, it thrives in dry, alkaline conditions that mimic its native habitat. The flowers appear after summer drought breaks with the first autumn rains, a Mediterranean timing cue; in humid climates, plant in very well-drained raised beds and avoid summer irrigation. Foliage persists through winter and disappears in late spring. An excellent companion to autumn crocus in the fall bulb garden.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in early spring or fall and replant immediately; hexastylis grows slowly to form dense mats and is best divided from established nursery plants. It requires consistent moisture, humus-rich acidic soil, and can colonize dry shade under oaks once established.

directions: Plant in humus-rich, acidic, well-drained to moist soil in full to part shade; Hexastylis arifolia (littlebrownjug, heartleaf) is a native evergreen groundcover of the southeastern United States closely related to wild ginger (Asarum) — the two genera were formerly combined. The thick, leathery, arrow-shaped to heart-shaped leaves are deep glossy green, often with silvery-gray marbled patterns, and remain attractive year-round even in the coldest winters within its range. The flowers are inconspicuous ground-level jugs (hence 'littlebrownjug') hidden beneath the foliage, relying on fungus gnats for pollination. It is superb for covering ground in dry to moist shade under deciduous oaks, pines, and other native trees where its slow-but-steady spread creates a dense, weed-suppressing mat. An important food plant for the pipevine swallowtail butterfly.

planting.note: Plant grafted or air-layered trees in warm, well-drained soil after all frost danger has passed; mamoncillo is strictly tropical and is damaged below 28°F. Grafted trees may begin fruiting in 5–8 years versus 10–15 years for seedlings. A single tree may produce fruit but two trees of different seedling origin improves fruiting.

directions: Plant in well-drained, fertile soil in full sun in frost-free climates; mamoncillo (Spanish lime, quenepa, genip) is a large tropical tree native to Central America and the Caribbean that produces clusters of golf-ball-sized green fruits with a brittle outer skin concealing a translucent, salmon-colored pulp clinging tightly to a large seed. The flavor is a unique combination — simultaneously sweet-tart, tropical, and faintly lime-like with a passionfruit quality — that is utterly distinctive among tropical fruits. Fruits are eaten by biting through the skin and sucking the pulp off the seed, which is the traditional way to consume them across the Caribbean. They are also made into juice, fermented into wine, and processed into candy. Mamoncillo is an important street-food fruit in Puerto Rico, Colombia, Cuba, and throughout the Caribbean, where it is eaten fresh out of hand in large quantities. The tree is drought-tolerant once established and very long-lived.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit,

planting.note: Root stem cuttings with at least one node in moist medium; monstera grows outdoors year-round only in frost-free climates and is damaged below 30°F. In zones 9 it can be grown in a sheltered location with frost protection but typically dies to the ground in cold winters and regrows from the roots.

directions: Root cuttings in moist mix and transplant to rich, well-drained soil in part shade; Monstera deliciosa (ceriman, Mexican breadfruit) is the beloved houseplant grown everywhere, but in frost-free zones 10–11 it grows outdoors as a vigorous climbing vine with its characteristic enormous, deeply split and hole-perforated leaves reaching 3 feet across, producing exotic 6–8 inch cone-shaped edible fruits that taste like a combination of banana, pineapple, and vanilla. The fruit takes over a year to ripen from flower to harvest — it ripens from the base upward as the hexagonal scale-like sections of the outer husk lift and fall away, revealing the white, creamy, flesh underneath. Unripe fruit contains oxalate crystals that cause severe mouth irritation; only fully ripe sections should be eaten. The flavor of ripe ceriman is exceptional and unlike any common fruit. It climbs vigorously by aerial roots and makes a dramatic specimen in tropical landscapes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Tropical Fruit
